data.title: FTI Consulting: Balanced Performance, Fair Valuation
data.description:
FTI Consulting, Inc. (NYSE:FCN) hasn’t really done much since I called it a hold back in July. The stock is slightly down but basically flat, and that’s unsurprising, given that I didn’t see any reasons to be bullish or bearish then, and not much has changed regarding my position now.

However, there are some important things I want to touch on in this update, hence the publication.

Q2 2025 Results

Here are FTI’s Q2 numbers:

  • Revenue was $944 million (basically flat from last year but ahead of expectations).

  • EPS was $2.13, down from $2.34 in Q2 2024, but still a beat.

  • Adjusted EBITDA margin held at just under 12%.

All things considered, I’d say that the results show us that the company is stable, and that’s important because some segments are facing real headwinds.

Going off my initial review of the segments, I’d say that there are two engines firing (CF&R and StratCom), one steady performer [FLC], and two still struggling (Econ and Tech). The mix balances out.

Looking at the segments now. Let me start with the big surprise, and then we’ll look at the rest. That bright surprise was Strategic Communications. Here, revenue jumped 21% YoY to a record $103 million, mostly driven by demand across corporate reputation, financial communications, and crisis work. The margins are also much improved, both on a YoY and sequential basis, so this business is now contributing in a way it hasn’t done for a while.

On to the rest of the segments, then, and pardon me if my language reads rather tersely from here. Corporate Finance and Restructuring had the standout quarter. Revenue hit a record $379 million, up 9% year over year, with restructuring up 25% and transactions up 10%. Segment margins climbed above 21%, so clearly, CF&R was still the engine of the quarter.

Forensic and Litigation grew 10% to $187 million, and most of that growth came from strong demand in financial services, cybersecurity, and construction solutions. Margins also nearly doubled from last year, and we can see how leverage is starting to work in that practice.

Economic Consulting is still the weak spot, which is unsurprising. Revenue fell 17% year over year, and segment margins collapsed to 7%. To be clear, some of that is tied to turnover and heavy forgivable loan costs, and management admits it will take time for the rebuild to pay off.

With Technology, the picture is much less pretty. Revenue dropped 28% to $84 million as fewer M&A “second requests” flowed through the pipeline. Again, it’s not unexpected, given that this is a cyclical business tied to deal volume and regulatory scrutiny, and right now both are lighter than a year ago. Still, it means we have to wait and see what deals like the BigID partnership add here over the next few quarters.

Capital Allocation, Management & Strategy

I think the most striking thing in Q2 was how aggressive FTI was on buybacks. They spent nearly $355 million repurchasing stock in the quarter, and $540 million total was spent in the first half. Obviously, this isn’t a token program; it’s a deliberate move to shrink the float and return cash. I can’t fault them for this because the company does have a lot of leverage left, although the timing is interesting. But it makes sense because the stock hasn’t really gone anywhere this year, and there’s value in leaning in.

FTI didn’t fund those buybacks with its own cash: they tapped debt, which isn’t something FTI usually does in size. Total debt is now around $470 million, which is a ~200% increase from just $160 million in Q1. It’s a big number, but as I mentioned earlier, leverage is still modest at around 1.2 times gross debt to EBITDA, and it’s closer to zero on a net basis when you adjust for cash. CFO Ajay Sabherwal made a point on the call that leverage is an outcome, not a target, and I think that’s telling. They aren’t trying to run the business to a ratio; they’re taking opportunities when they see them, whether that’s buybacks, acquisitions, or hiring talent.

Now, that point about hiring is important because the company is doubling down on it even while Econ and Tech are soft. Economic Consulting is suffering from high turnover and slower demand, yet management is bringing in senior academics and industry experts. Those forgivable loans show up as costs now, but I believe that the bet is that in a year or two, those people will be anchoring major engagements. That’s the same playbook they’ve run for years: invest when others pull back. It has hurt the margins in Q2, but the long-term logic is clear.

Company CEO Steven Gunby called 2025 a tough year (I think proving is more like it), and he’s right. This is not a growth year by any stretch. We have the thriving segments offset by the struggling ones, and that balance is the heart of the strategy.

The other thing I notice is how disciplined FTI has stayed on its positioning. The company is leaning into areas where trust and expertise are everything; think bankruptcies, disputes, regulatory events, and crises. That’s why the investments in senior people matter more than the near-term hit to margins. It’s also why management can afford to be patient when Tech or Econ hits a rough patch.

A Small Note On The CFO Transition

Last month, FTI announced that Ajay Sabherwal, who has been its CFO since 2016, would step down in September to take a role outside consulting. Paul Linton, the current Chief Strategy and Transformation Officer, will serve as interim CFO while the company searches for a permanent replacement. Linton isn’t a stranger to FTI: he’s been with the firm for a decade and already runs major transformation initiatives, so I believe that the handoff should be smooth enough.

Even so, having your CFO leave at this point adds some uncertainty, in my view. Sabherwal has been at the heart of FTI’s fiscal discipline, particularly on capital allocation, and I can’t stress how important his role is, especially with the rate at which the firm leaned into heavy buybacks this year. I don’t think that the transition will affect much in the long term, but I’ll keep an eye on it.

No Rerating In The Near Term

At around $163 a share, the stock is trading at roughly 22 times forward earnings, about 14 times EBITDA, and around 1.6 times sales. Those numbers are almost exactly where they were when I last wrote about the company in July. It’s a bit surprising, given that the company has taken 3.3 million shares out of circulation in the current fiscal year.

That tells me the market is pricing FTI for what it is, and investors aren’t expecting explosive growth, but they are willing to pay a premium for resilience, cash generation, and management discipline. I think the multiple is a bit higher than you’d see for cyclical advisory firms, but it’s also lower than fast-growing consulting names.

As I said in July, I don’t see a case for rerating higher until there’s a clearer recovery in Tech and Economic Consulting. Those segments have historically driven growth in up cycles, and right now they’re still dragging. Without a turnaround there, or a step-change in overall earnings power, the current valuation already reflects the strength of Corporate Finance, FLC, and StratCom.

data.title: Bristol Myers Squibb: Buy This Deep Bargain Before The Market Wakes To Income
data.description:
The stock market doesn’t always follow a straight path, and that’s a great thing for value investors. That’s because ups and downs enable us to pick up quality companies at bargain prices. In the words of football great Jerry Rice: “Today I will do what other won’t, so tomorrow I can accomplish what others can’t”. At present, such opportunities exist amongst a number of dividend stocks in the pharmaceutical, real estate, and energy sectors.

This brings me to Bristol Myers Squibb (NYSE:BMY), which I last covered back in October 2024, highlighting its attractive valuation and solid growth prospects, particularly in the neuroscience space. BMY currently trades in deep value territory at the current price of $45 with a forward PE of 7x and a dividend yield well over 5%. As shown below, it sits close to the 52-week low of $43 from the end of July.

In this article, I revisit BMY including recent business results, and discuss why it’s currently a deep bargain for income and potentially strong upside potential from here, so let’s get started!

Why BMY?

Bristol Myers Squibb is a leading global biopharmaceutical company, with therapies that support oncology, hematology, cardiovascular, immunology, and neuroscience. BMY’s portfolio includes blockbuster drugs such as Opdivo and Eliquis, as well as an emerging growth including Camzyos, Breyanzi, Reblozyl, and newly launched products Cobenfy and Qvantig.

BMY’s strategy is centered on advancing innovative therapies and expanding through partnerships. This includes its global strategic partnership with BioNTech to develop bispecific therapy that could be a new standard of care for multiple tumor types. Partnerships like these position BMY well for the next wave of immune-oncology innovation.

Notably, the customized oncology therapy market is expected to see strong growth at an 8.1% CAGR between now and the end of this decade. As shown below, this market is expected to grow from $127 billion this year to $202 billion by 2030.

Contrary to what BMY’s lackluster share price performance may suggest, it produced steady results during Q2 2025. This is reflected by global net sales growth of 1% YoY (0% on an FX-neutral basis) to $12.3 billion. This was driven by robust results from the growth portfolio, which saw an 18% YoY increase in sales (17% FX-neutral). This helped to offset the decline in the legacy portfolio. BMY’s gross margin was 73%, which is higher than the 72% guidance due to favorable product mix. As shown below, BMY maintains an A+ grade for profitability with EBITDA and Net Income margins of 40% and 10.6%, respectively.

Specifically, the oncology portfolio demonstrated solid results, with Opdivo growing by 7% YOY to reach $2.56 billion in sales. Yervoy and Opdualag both saw strong 15% and 20% YoY growth in sales. Notably, Qvantig debuted with $30 million in sales with positive feedback from patients and providers on benefits of treatment efficiencies from subcutaneous (under the skin) injection.

Also encouraging, Reblozyl (for treatment of anemia) has become a blockbuster drug for BMY, with it surpassing 1 billion in year-to-date sales. This is after 30% and 46% YoY growth in the U.S. and internationally during the second quarter. This was driven by demand across new indications and manufacturing improvements.

Moreover, Camzyos (for cardiomyopathy) growth continues to surge, growing by 86% YoY globally to $260 million due to strong demand. As shown below, Camzyos combined with legacy drug Eliquis make for a strong combination in the cardiovascular portfolio, with both demonstrating growth during the second quarter.

Encouragingly, management raised full-year revenue guidance by $700 million from the prior forecast to $47 billion at the midpoint of range. This is driven by strong-than-expected performance from the legacy portfolio. This includes Revlimid, which is now projected to deliver $3 billion in sales for this year.

BMY also has a strong pipeline, which includes the aforementioned partnership with BioNTech to develop a new standard of care for multiple tumor types. The pipeline includes 14 drugs for treatment of atrial fibrillation, pulmonary fibrosis, and multiple myeloma.

Importantly, BMY has a lifecycle expansion opportunity for Cobenfy in Alzheimer’s related psychosis. This presents a multibillion dollar opportunity if trial results deliver expected outcomes. Due to a growing aging population, the Alzheimer’s market is expected to grow at a robust pace at a 20% CAGR through the end of this decade to land at $16 billion, as shown below.

BMY carries a strong balance sheet with an ‘A’ credit rating from S&P. This is supported by an impressive $13.6 billion in cash and short-term investments on hand, and a safe net debt to TTM EBITDA ratio of 1.94x. This further supports the 5.5% dividend yield, which is well-protected by a 37% payout ratio. The dividend also comes with a 5-year CAGR of 7% and 17 consecutive years, putting BMY on a path to potentially becoming a Dividend Aristocrat.

Lastly, BMY appears to be a deep bargain at the current price of $45.02 with a forward PE of just 6.9. This means that BMY could return meaningful value to shareholders just by buying back its own shares, equating to a 14.4% earnings yield. As shown below, BMY’s forward PE sits far below its historical PE of 17.7.

At the current valuation, the market appears to have baked in a perpetual decline in earnings for BMY. I don’t believe that to be a realistic scenario considering its pipeline of opportunities supported by a strong balance sheet. At the current valuation, BMY would only need to deliver low to mid-single digit EPS growth annually to rise to a high single-digit PE valuation. This, combined with the 5.5% dividend yield could result in a base case scenario of market-beating total returns in the near term.

Risks to BMY include the fact that legacy drugs still represent a significant 46% of total revenue (down from 54% in the prior year period). This means that the growth portfolio will continue to need to demonstrate growth to offset legacy declines. Moreover, there is risk that pipeline drugs may not live up to their promise should clinical trials don’t produce the needed results. Disruptions to supply chains and/or higher tariffs could eat into margins.

Investor Takeaway

Bristol Myers Squibb stands out as a compelling deep value play in today’s volatile market. It offers a combination of income and strong upside potential from the current valuation. BMY continues to produce steady results, driven by double-digit growth in its next-gen portfolio and blockbuster performances from drugs like Reblozyl and Camzyos.

It has a robust pipeline, strategic partnerships and expanding opportunities in high-growth areas like oncology and Alzheimer’s. This makes BMY positioned for long-term relevance. Lastly, BMY is backed by a strong balance sheet, an A credit rating, and a secure 5.5% dividend yield. As such, BMY at the current price offers value investors a chance to buy a quality business at a steep discount. For these reasons, I currently rate BMY as a ‘Strong Buy’.

data.title: AT&T And Orange: Same Same But Different
data.description:
Back in May, I wrote an article "A Tale of Two Telco's" comparing two very different Telco stocks, AT&T (NYSE:T) and Orange (OTC:ORANY).

The tile was inspired by the classic novel, "A tale of two cities" by Charles Dickens, which contrasts the very different cites of London and Paris at the time of the French Revolution. The novel shows the differences between the two, while also highlighting what they had in common.

AT&T needs little introduction, being a market leader in the US market, and a favourite on Seeking Alpha. Orange, telecom leader in France, is less well known, but in my mind makes an interesting comparison.

In a nutshell, my conclusion was that while the pair have similarities, in terms of low growth, high dividend stocks with leading home market market shares, Orange makes an interesting alternative investment for part of a telecom allocation. This is due to a much lower valuation, and stronger growth opportunities in emerging markets in Eastern Europe and Africa.

I'd encourage readers to click on the link above to get up to speed with the details. There are also some interesting points raised in the commentary around some risks specific to Orange.

In this article I check back in on the latest developments with both companies, look at current market valuations, and revisit the thesis.

AT&T

Q2 earnings were released in July.

Overall, they showed stable but unspectacular progress, with some revenue growth in the Mobility segment, at 3.5% roughly keeping track with inflation, and good traction in Fiber which produced a 19% year on year revenue growth. The latter of course is needed to continue to capitalise on the massive investments in the network.

The numbers were equally unspectacular, but positive. Revenue overall was up 1.1%, while free cash flow reduced from $4.6bn to $4.4bn.

Adjusted EBITDA was up 3.5% year on year, again, roughly in line with inflation.

Adjusted earnings per share were flat at $0.52.

As the exhibit below shows, this improvement year on year is a turn lower than the 4.5% yoy improvement achieved in the previous quarter.

Net income of $4,861m was a big jump up year on year from $3,949, a 23% improvement.

There was continued progress to chip away at the debt, which reduced by $6.5 bn year on year to $120 bn, but ticked up by $1 bn quarter on quarter. The normal quarterly $2 bn dividend was supplemented by a $1 bn buyback in Q2.

So basically, the company borrowed $1 bn to return $1 bn in buybacks.

2025 guidance is for EBITDA growth broadly in line with inflation, and stable free cash flow of around $4 bn per quarter.

EchoStar

In August, AT&T announced the acquisition of $23 bn of unused broadband spectrum from EchoStar, with the deal expected to close in mid 2026. The debt funding for this is expected to take the debt to EBITDA ratio back up to 3x, with the plan being to return this to the 2.5x target range by mid 2029.

At the same time, the company reiterated its planned $20 bn capacity to return capital to shareholders from 2025-2027.

It will be interesting to see how this pans out. There is no guarantee of how much the $23 bn in capacity can be converted into new margins, or how long this will take.

Management commented during the conference call that their own predictions have the deal being fully accretive after three years.

Specifically, we expect it will drive incremental service revenue and EBITDA within the first 24 months following deal close, with accretion to adjusted EPS and free cash flow expected in year 3

There is no explanation on how the math work to have the debt to EBITDA multiple reduce from 3x to 2.5x within four years alongside a $20 bn buyback and only a single year of incremental cash flow contribution. According to GuruFocus, net debt to EBITDA is running at 2.89x as at Q2.

On the call, the question was answered directionally around debt reduction via:

...a combination of accelerated EBITDA growth plus deleverage debt reduction as a result of the strong free cash flows beginning in 2028 and beyond.

I for one would love to see further details behind these assertions.

The market reaction seems to have been generally neutral to negative on the earnings, and generally mildly positive on the spectrum acquisition. AT&T has outperformed rival Verizon (VZ) since the announcement.

Orange

Orange reported Q2 around the same time as AT&T.

Similar to AT&T, results were solid but not earth-shattering with a 3.8% increase on adjusted EBITDA, and 7.7% on operating cash flow. Guidance was slightly uplifted on improved margins in France and continued growth in Mid East and Africa (MEA).

A word on adjusted EBITDA which is reported as EBITDAal. This adds is adjustments for discontinued business, and critically, Labour costs. The latter refers not to normal salaries but to training and other employment provisions. The legacy French business has been subject to significant pressure from their unionised workforce, which is referred to as GEPP. An agreement was reached with the unions in February, as reported here. This entails significant investment and pension provisions from Orange, which had not been quantified until Q2. The impact of this on net income is discussed below.

The revenue picture was not great. A 2.2% reduction in top line in France was offset by a strong 12.8% increase in MEA.

Bottom line shows a more compelling story. A strong focus on bottom line in France and Europe has generated good margin growth on shrinking top line. in MEA the margins have been maintained with revenues feeding proportionately into earnings. All segments except Orange Business are contributing to the 3.8% margin growth.

Guidance for EBITDAal was affirmed, with the slightest of positive spins. Leverage of 2x EBITDAal. There is also a commitment to a floor on the dividend per share, equating to about 5.48% yield at current prices on the native ticker.

This all looks fine, however the GAAP net income figures are dominated by a large provision for GEPP that was crystallised in the quarter. This wipes out the EUR 1.2 bn half year net income.

While this agreement clearly has a major negative impact on the current year results, resolution of this longstanding dispute does provide clarity on the impact, and hopefully removes the headwind of potential labour issues for the coming years.

This certainly seems to be the view of analysts, who have downgraded their EPS estimate for the current year to EUR 0.65, but upgraded 2026 expectations to EUR 1.5 per share.

This implied a 50% payout ratio for the 2026 dividend.

Management had the option to fund the EUR 1.2bn GEPP wholly or partly from the EUR 1.2bn shareholder dividend; however, instead this was maintained, and the debt increased by EUR 800m from the end of 2024.

All in all, the market seems to buy the deal, with a sharp price increase of up to 12% following the earnings release. The incremental increase of debt keeps Orange under the 2x target threshold, and the provision seems to be viewed as bearable.

Share Price Performance

Since the original article, both stocks have risen by a similar amount, and total returns have also mapped each other. Given the Orange half year net loss resulting from the GEPP, this is remarkable.

I did a quick cross check with the native ticker in EUR. Since earnings of 28th July, the native price has increased by 4.4%, compared to 5.68% for the ADR. I put the 1.3% difference mostly down to currency, which is discussed below.

Valuations

With 2025 earnings, as discussed, crushed by the GEPP provision, the 2025 forward price earnings multiple for Orange has shot up from 10x to 25x since my original article. AT&T has increased mildly from 13.5x to 14x over the same period, with broadly stable forward earnings and some price appreciation.

Looking further into current analyst 2026 earnings estimates, the script flips back again, with AT&T trading at 13.3x and Orange at 10.6x 2026 earnings.

On a price to free cash flow basis, the relative valuations on this metric have flipped as well, with AT&T now looking better priced on TTM cash flow.

The cause here was a lump in new licence fees at Orange reducing free cash flow by 40% in the half year. No outlook is given by Orange for whether future periods will be impacted by further licence payments.

Comments To The Previous Article.

In the original article, I suggested that it might make sense for some investors to trim some AT&T allocation and diversify into Orange. I'd like to address some of the comments people made, which were mostly about Orange.

Withholding Tax

Investors should be aware that Orange dividends paid to non French residents are subject to a 25% withholding tax, which investors can recover at tax time under the relevant double taxation agreements.

Dividend Frequency

Orange dividends are paid half-yearly while AT&T pays quarterly. This is quite common for European stocks, and can affect investors cash flows. However, this should not be an issue with a modest allocation to Orange.

Currency Risk

Readers raised concerns about the EUR/USD currency risk. In my mind this is one of the reasons to consider diversification into Orange. The USD remains at elevated levels compared to the past. The current administration is enacting policies that seem targeted at devaluing the USD. These include continued debt issuance and reduced taxation with a rising fiscal deficit and pressure on the Fed to cut policy rates.

As the 20 year currency chart from Chartoasis.com shows, the EUR has recently been at lows, and has started to move up against the USD, along with other major currencies. Current policies would expect to put continued pressure on the EUR/USD, and I'd expect to see an equilibrium rate of around USD 1.3 to EUR emerge in the coming years.

Granted, the Eurozone has some fiscal issues to deal with; however, none of this is new, and the U.S. debt burden is likely to continue to drive the USD lower vs the EUR.

Political Risk

Orange has had issues with unions, which is behind the large GEPP settlement. Clearly this has been a negative for investors in 2025. The open question is whether the GEPP is sufficient to resolve this. It should also be noted that the French government is a shareholder of Orange, holding about a quarter of the float.

The recent development for the US government to take a 10% holding in Intel is a development to watch in this context. This looks like a first step in building a Sovereign wealth fund in the U.S. Back to my "Tale of Two Cities" theme - similarities can exist even where political ideology differs.

Conclusion And How I Am Playing It

Similarities

Both are telecom companies with leading positions in their local markets. Both have low growth expectations in the 3% range. Both companies are investing to support this growth. Both companies offer a decent dividend, in the 4-5% range. The AT&T earnings payout ratio is lower, but both are supported well by long term cash flows.

Differences

Orange is executing growth via rapid expansion into emerging markets in Eastern Europe and Africa. AT&T has invested heavily into new bandwidth in the U.S.

Here I see competitive challenges to AT&T, as in Verizon (VZ) and other mature competitors, consumers have very credible alternatives.

In its emerging market strategy, Orange has proved its ability to generate strong growth even in a competitive pricing environment. I believe that it's maturity gives it an edge in these markets.

AT&T performance has been consistent, while Orange has made a large provision that has wiped out half its 2025 net income. This provision is expected to be a one off.

Both have a large debt burden; however, Orange is running at 2x adjusted EBITDA, while AT&T is closer to 3x.

Debt was used by AT&T to fund a buyback, while at Orange, debt supported the dividend payout this half.

Orange is exposed to the political risk of France; however, offers currency diversification and emerging market growth. AT&T is "USA all the way" with the respective risks and advantages.

Orange is around 30% cheaper than AT&T on forward earnings.

All in all, I continue to see a place for Orange in my dividend portfolio to complement and diversify my AT&T holding. However, I acknowledge the risks, and have elected to gain exposure via short puts. I have Sep 19th OTM puts expiring today (on the native ticker). I am looking to replace these with March 2026 EUR 12.6 puts which last traded at an annualised 5.5% yield, and are around 8% OTM.

I rate both stocks as Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Realty Income Vs. W. P. Carey: Which Is The Better REIT To Buy Today?
data.description:
REITs (VNQ) are still priced at historically high yields today.

Interestingly, even the "blue chip REITs" like Realty Income Corporation (NYSE:O) and W. P. Carey Inc. (NYSE:WPC) weren't immune to the crash, and that's despite:

  • Owning class A properties.
  • Enjoying steady rent growth.
  • And having strong investment-grade rated balance sheets.

As a result, they are now priced at historically high dividend yields:

Realty Income W. P. Carey
Dividend Yield 5.4% 5.3%

But which one is the best to buy today?

I believe that both are attractive at these valuations, but if I had to pick one, it would be W. P. Carey. Here are three reasons why:

Reason #1: W. P. Carey Enjoys CPI-Based Rent Increases

Both Realty Income and W. P. Carey are net lease REITs. As such, they both enjoy very long leases at ~10 years and steady annual rent hikes.

But there's a big difference in how their rents are hiked.

Realty Income has mostly fixed hikes in its leases, limiting its annual growth to 1-2% per year in most cases.

W. P. Carey, on the other hand, enjoys mostly CPI-based rent hikes, which are far better, especially in an inflationary world.

This has resulted in far faster rent growth in the past years:

Realty Income W. P. Carey
Rent Growth ~1.5% ~3%

We remain in a very uncertain world, and I value the protection of CPI-based rent hikes just in case we get another spike in inflation.

It reduces risks and also increases the organic growth potential.

Reason #2: Realty Income Is Getting Too Big For Its Own Good

Unfortunately, I fear that Realty Income won't be able to make up for its slower organic growth by growing faster externally.

External growth is when you raise capital to buy more properties. If your cost of capital is lower than the expected returns of your new acquisitions, then there is a positive spread that will result in growth on a "per-share" basis, despite the expanding total share count.

Realty Income has excelled at this in the past, and this is one of the main reasons why it has performed so well over the long run. It has been able to consistently raise capital, buy properties, pocket the spread, and grow, grow, grow...

But unfortunately, this has its limits.

The company has gotten huge, and as a result, every new investment now has a relatively small impact on its bottom line.

Realty Income W. P. Carey
Market Cap $55 billion $14.8 billion

It has to find billions of properties to acquire each year just to keep the ball rolling. This lowers its ability to be selective, and it also loses bargaining power with property sellers.

W. P. Carey, on the other hand, is still much smaller in size relative to Realty Income, and this should allow it to target a faster external growth rate over the long run.

Reason #3: W. P. Carey Has Clear Catalysts For Multiple Expansion

Both REITs are today discounted, and their valuations are not materially different:

Realty Income W. P. Carey
P/FFO 14x 15x
Dividend Yield 5.4% 5.3%

I think that both will enjoy some upside in the coming years as interest rates are cut and their valuation multiples recover.

However, I think that W. P. Carey has a clearer path to unlocking this upside because it has clear catalysts.

The two main catalysts are the following:

1) The continued growth in its industrial exposure

Already today, the company generates 64% of its revenue from industrial properties, and we expect this to rise to over 70% already in the coming years.

I think that this rising exposure to industrial properties will improve its market sentiment and be rewarded with a higher FFO multiple.

The market loves industrial REITs, and it is today commonly pricing them at >20x FFO. W. P. Carey is still priced at the much lower multiple of a diversified net lease REIT, but over time, its multiple will likely lean closer to that of an industrial REIT as it keeps increasing this exposure.

Realty Income, on the other hand, is mostly invested in retail net lease properties and does not have much exposure to industrial properties.

I think that its properties are less desirable, especially in the context of a trade war and a weakening economy.

2) The acceleration of its growth

Another reason why W. P. Carey is today discounted because the market sees it as a "no-growth income REIT."

But there are good reasons why its FFO per share hasn't grown much in recent years. First, it spent years unwinding its asset management business, which was dilutive to its FFO per share. Then it spun off its office properties into a separate REIT and used a good chunk of the properties to pay off, which was dilutive as well.

However, this is now in the past, and W. P. Carey has become a higher-quality REIT with stronger growth prospects as a result of this transformation.

As its growth now accelerates, we expect its market sentiment to improve as its perception changes from that of a "diversified REIT with no growth" to that of a "growing industrial REIT." Its valuation multiple could expand drastically in the process.

Realty Income does not enjoy similar catalysts to improve its market sentiment.

Bottom Line

Both REITs are compelling today.

But if I had to pick one, it would be W. P. Carey, and that is why we own a position in it at High Yield Landlord.

data.title: Why Over 40% Of My Dividend Portfolio Is In This One Sector
data.description:
The standard wisdom about diversification is usually correct.

Warren Buffett once said that diversification is the only free lunch in investing. And yet, many of the world's best investors tend to maintain quite concentrated portfolios.

Personally, I like the idea of weighting holdings by conviction, even if that means being a little more concentrated than a financial advisor would recommend for the average investor.

Right now, over 40% of my dividend growth portfolio is concentrated in one sector. I don't intend for that to be the case forever, but I think it makes a lot of sense for right now. Let me explain why.

Here's the agenda for this week:

  • Some fresh charts and discussion on the state of "the American consumer," which these days primarily means affluent Americans.
  • More charts on the extreme bifurcation in the stock market, which may be justified by a similar bifurcation in margins between tech and non-tech businesses. Then again, if the circular flow of AI-related spending within the tech space doesn't expand out to include the broader economy, maybe the bubble fears are warranted.
  • Why I have over 40% of my portfolio concentrated in one sector about which I'm being purposely taciturn.
  • Some commentary on my 6-stock buy list.

Onward!

Which American Consumer?

The American consumer is still strong!

Wait, which American consumer?

The renter living paycheck-to-paycheck with no stock exposure or the 60-year-old homeowner earning six figures with a ballooning 401k?

As I've explained many times in the past, the "American consumer" is really the average of two different sets of consumers: the Paycheck-to-Paycheck ("P2P") consumer and the Affluent consumer.

As the Moody's Analytics and others have demonstrated recently, consumer spending, which drives 2/3rds of the US economy, is overwhelmingly fueled by Affluent Americans. P2P consumers only affect aggregate consumption slightly around the edges, because the bulk of their spending isn't discretionary.

So, when we see total consumer spending growth slowing down, it mainly means that the Affluent are reining it in a little bit.

The above is just a year-to-date chart.

Zooming out to the last five years, we find that, sure enough, the top 20% of income earners have accounted for the vast majority of the real (inflation-adjusted) growth in consumption.

That chart comes from Moody's Analytics, which also demonstrated earlier this year that the top 10% of earners account for just shy of half of all consumer spending.

But this information is corroborated by data from Bank of America, which shows that from early 2024 to the present, growth in credit and debit card spending has increasingly come from higher-income households.

Meanwhile, growth in credit and debit card spending from lower-income households has become anemic. And this is not inflation-adjusted. Therefore, lower-income household spending now appears to be growing slower than the rate of inflation.

This is a bit unexpected, because one might expect a strong economy combined with deportations of low-skilled immigrants to be favorable to lower-income Americans.

It appears that although AI may someday be significantly more labor-replacing than it is today, high-skilled Americans are so far benefiting from higher productivity by earning higher wages, which in turn facilitates continued growth in consumption.

Meanwhile, the other big winner among the spectrum of consumers are stockholders, most of whom are older. It's no surprise, then, to see that older Americans account for an increasing share of consumption.

Over the coming decades for the rest of the century, retirement-aged Americans (65+) will command an increasingly higher share of total spending.

What does this mean for investors?

It can mean multiple things. It can mean that the US economy is remarkably resilient, because the Affluent will likely continue to spend money as long as they have ample money to spend. It can also mean the US economy is dangerously dependent on a relatively small number of consumers.

It's noteworthy, though, that US retail sales growth tends to be pretty steady in the low- to mid-single-digits through all but the severest of recessions.

Notice that in 2008-2009 (the Great Financial Crisis), nominal retail sales didn't dip until after the recession started.

Also notice that retail sales growth saw a nice bump in the late 1990s and into 2000, coinciding with the Dot Com bubble. After the bursting of the bubble, retail sales growth declined alongside the major stock indices as the wealth effect reversed.

Bottom line:

  1. As long as high-income Americans continue to be employed, they will continue to spend.
  2. As long as the stock market continues to rip, older Americans with large stock portfolios will also continue to spend.
  3. As long as these two groups continue to spend, the US economy will most likely stay out of recession.
  4. AI-related layoffs of white collar workers and/or a significant stock market downturn could cause affluent Americans to substantially cut back on spending.

Which Stock Market?

The stock market is booming!

Wait, which stocks?

Just as for "the American consumer," there's a sharp contrast between the average public company performance and that of AI-related stocks. Generally speaking, AI stocks (CHAT) are ripping, whereas your average stock that would fall into a value index (VTV) is performing far more modestly.

I've included the equal-weight tech ETF (RSPT) in the above chart to show that it isn't really the tech sector as a whole that's rip-roaring higher right now. It's just those AI-related stocks. If you excluded AI-related stocks, the rest of the tech sector is not performing much better than the average value stock this year.

That makes it increasingly deceptive when the financial media talks about "the stock market" — as if all stocks were the same.

That is true also of average stock market operating margins.

This year, the average public company operating margin has been surging higher this year, breaking hard with nominal GDP growth.

This is a historical anomaly. It basically never happens.

But here, again, the tyranny of averages conceals great disparity.

Observe in the chart below the disparity in profit margins between tech and non-tech stocks:

Both operating and profit margins have slowly crept higher for non-tech stocks over the last two or three decades. But for tech stocks? Operating margins have more than doubled, while profit margins have roughly quadrupled.

While I don't have the data to prove it, I would surmise that the expansion of margins in the S&P 500 this year is due almost entirely to AI-related companies.

What I do have the data to show is that the technology sector (XLK) is enjoying strong, double-digit sales growth this year, while the average of all other sectors are seeing sales growth below 5%.

While you can't argue with revenue growth, it must be stressed that most of this AI-related growth in tech sales so far has been driven by a circular ecosystem of spending within the tech space.

Software developers spend money on cloud computing. Hyperscalers spend money on data centers. Data centers spend money on semiconductors. And so on.

This massive deployment of capital by the hyperscalers is doing the most work to churn money around in this AI ecosystem.

But what if these AI companies spend all this money to throw a party and then no one shows up?

While more and more businesses had been using AI in some way to support their business this year, something changed this summer. Bigger companies (the 100-249 employee group as well as the 250+ employee group) have been pulling back on usage of AI.

Does this relate to the recent MIT study showing that the vast majority of AI products employed by businesses did not generate a positive ROI?

Could it be that, while individual workers may find AI chatbots helpful and productivity-enhancing, the myriad AI products intended to be implemented into team workflows are not terribly helpful or productivity-enhancing?

It seems plausible to me that someday, AI software products will be so good that businesses and individuals will be lining up and saying "take my money!"

But it also seems plausible that commercially viable uses of AI turn out to be fewer and less lucrative than the tech sector is currently preparing for.

Why 40%+ of My Portfolio Is In One Sector Today

Over 40% of my dividend growth portfolio is invested in commercial real estate ("CRE"), namely in REITs.

That may sound like incredibly reckless concentration into one sector, but I think that it is justified in the current environment. Don't get me wrong. Eventually, I'd like to diversify my portfolio. But for now, I'm confident that the 40%+ of my portfolio in REITs are well-positioned for strong performance for the foreseeable future.

Lots of critics have told me that REITs are dead money, that they're chronic underperformers, and that they are worthless in today's environment.

I think that's all recency bias.

Compare the total return performance of the Vanguard Real Estate Fund (VGSIX) to the S&P 500 (SPY) since 1996:

Notice that publicly traded CRE has spent most of the last three decades outperforming the SPY. The only two periods that the SPY has significantly outperformed CRE are the Dot Com bubble era of the late 1990s and the AI boom from 2023 to the present.

It wasn't merely lower interest rates that fueled REITs' strong performance up to 2022, although interest rates played a role.

The arguably bigger factor was favorable supply-demand dynamics. For most of the time since 1996, most sectors of CRE enjoyed faster growth in tenant demand than growth in supply of new space. That fueled a virtuous cycle of high occupancy, rent and cash flow growth, and accretive portfolio expansion.

In addition to a spike in interest rates, CRE suffered the strong headwind from 2022-2024 of huge growth in new supply.

The combination of ultra-low interest rates and massive fiscal stimulus in 2020-2021 fueled a huge development boom. Those new buildings started competing for tenants starting in 2022-2023, depending on the CRE sub-sector (some types of buildings take longer to complete than others).

You can see this massive spike in completions, peaking in 2024, for the multifamily sub-sector:

There are similar charts for every other CRE sector — especially industrial and life science — that saw a spike in construction after the pandemic. They all look the same. Today, construction starts are falling, development pipelines are shrinking, and the peaks in completions/deliveries are now in the rearview mirror.

You can see this also in total building permits (a leading indicator of future development), which have now declined 5 months in a row — the longest streak of contraction since the Great Financial Crisis.

The longer this contraction in building permits goes on, the more the CRE development pipeline will shrink.

And the more the CRE development pipeline shrinks, the less new competing space will come to market to compete with existing supply in the future.

So now, not only is the interest rate headwind fading, so also is the headwind of excessive new supply.

All else being equal, then, it looks like we are returning to a macro backdrop far more favorable to REITs than the last three and a half years.

My Buy List Today

Turning to my buy list, I'll note that four of the six dividend growth stocks I hope to buy next week are high-quality REITs. Many REITs have already rallied above the points where I recently bought shares, but these four remain eminently buyable, in my view.

Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
Agree Realty (ADC) 4.3% Mid-Single-Digit
American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) 3.6% Mid-Single-Digit
American Tower (AMT) 3.5% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
H2O America (HTO) 3.4% Mid-Single-Digit
Permian Resources (PR) 4.4% Mid-Single-Digit
Public Storage (PSA) 4.2% Mid-Single-Digit

As regular readers know, I like compounders. They are the steady, reliable rowers of my portfolio, powering the growth of my passive income via regular dividend increases.

These are all extraordinary compounders, in my view.

Agree Realty

ADC exudes quality in both its portfolio of single-tenant retail properties, tenanted overwhelmingly by the largest and strongest retailers in the nation, and in its low-leveraged and simple balance sheet, which recently earned its first A- credit rating from Fitch. Consistency is the name of the game for ADC, and it is working. The REIT has raised more than enough capital to complete all of its ambitious acquisition guidance for this year without needing to raise another penny. On top of all that, insiders have been buying recently.

American Tower

I suspect that for both ADC and AMT, the recent underperformance is at least partially related to fading fears of a near-future recession. Both REITs are highly defensive and recession-resistant.

But both REITs will also benefit from lower interest rates, which makes their lack of positive stock price performance recently somewhat baffling.

To be fair, AMT has been hit by fears that SpaceX's purchase of spectrum from EchoStar (~2% of AMT's total revenue) will lead to more churn, which strikes me as unlikely. If anything, it appears to be simply a transfer of spectrum from EchoStar to SpaceX, who will continue to utilize space on AMT's towers. Even if there is churn, though, it looks to me like it will be minimal.

American Homes 4 Rent

As for AMH, this high-quality single-family rental REIT has been a melting ice cube lately because of reports that homeowners are becoming "accidental landlords" by renting out their homes after moving rather than selling. This, so say the reports, is creating a large amount of "shadow supply" in many SFR markets.

I suspect these reports are true but overblown. Yes, SFR vacancy rates have been creeping higher this year, but recent SFR reports like this one signal stability in rent growth and rental demand through August. Only low-end SFRs, which are not AMH's focus, are seeing a drift lower in fundamentals.

Also, in addition to the charts above, see also this chart showing the decline in total US housing starts over the last several years.

As population growth slows (and unless net immigration picks back up at some point), this metric needs to keep coming down.

H2O America

As for HTO, this multi-state water utility company has a very long — 57 year — dividend growth record, which indicates the defensiveness of its business model and the financial conservatism of management. The balance sheet is in good shape and enjoys an A- credit rating, which keeps debt costs reasonably low.

Permian Resources

The price of oil appears to have settled in a range of low- to mid-$60s, which is just fine for PR. The Permian oil & gas producer's crack management team have indicated that, due to cost-saving measures, they should be able to generate as much free cash flow this year as last year even at an oil price of about $63.

Leverage is low, and PR's management team (who are large shareholders themselves) have proven themselves shrewd allocators of capital. Nevertheless, PR is an oil stock, and oil stocks are indomitably (and, perhaps, understandably) tied to the price of oil.

Public Storage

PSA has gone precisely nowhere this year (except, as of this writing, down about 5%). That's probably because the housing market is still largely frozen, preventing the sort of housing turnover that tends to create the need for temporary storage space. In any case, PSA is still one of the largest and most efficient operators in this space, and its low-leveraged balance sheet boasts an enviable A credit rating. The 4.2%-yielding dividend is rock-solid and will return to growth whenever the REIT's bottom line does.

data.title: All-Time Highs Are Not Necessarily Reasons To Sell
data.description:
Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to evaluate the broader investment backdrop with a specific focus on US stocks. The reason behind my writing this at this precise moment is that the major indices are hitting record levels. There has been a tremendous amount of bullishness feeding the equity market after we saw the sell-off in April, and this upswing has found renewed momentum as traders are betting on the rate-cutting cycle to get underway from the Fed:

This has, of course, been welcome news for investors. But it is giving me pause, to say the least, and I'm sure it has given others some pause as well. When I see the phrase "all-time high," it does make me wonder if a pullback is around the corner. This can lead to caution that winds up not being beneficial and leaves investors missing out on more gains that often follow these headlines.

In this review I will tackle a few reasons why making buy or sell decisions simply because a large index has hit a record may not always be the best move. In most cases it makes sense for retail investors to simply "stay the course" and remain committed to their long-term plan. There is historical evidence this is the right approach, and I will discuss why in detail below.

**I am long the major US indices through the Invesco NASDAQ QQQ 100 ETF (QQQM), the SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF Trust (NYSEARCA:DIA), and the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:VOO).

New Highs Are Surprisingly Common

My first point is to put this reality into perspective. An "all-time high" sounds great - and almost unique to a degree. But how uncommon is it? Well, for those who have been following markets as long as I have (or longer), we have found that all-time highs actually happen more often than one might believe. This is because the market has been pulled upward over time in a fairly consistent manner. It makes "all-time highs" a regular attribute of a bull market, and this has certainly been true over the last decade:

What I am driving at here is that "highs" are not actually that rare. The extension of that is that just because we hit a new high doesn't mean one needs to automatically make adjustments. Can you? Of course - and maybe you should. But as a general rule, that would make you a fairly active trader if you used a new high as an opportunity to trade every single time. Just looking at how many times we hit a new year in the past year would leave someone with quite a bit of trading if one did indeed use this as a time to pull the trigger.

This isn't to say that you shouldn't ever trade because a high is reached. Maybe you breached your risk tolerance level. Or maybe your portfolio has gotten tilted too aggressively towards one idea, thesis, or index. Fair enough. I certainly use metrics like that to make adjustments, and when one index hits a high, that could mean things have gotten out of balance. But the conclusion more broadly is that just because the market is hitting highs doesn't mean that we need to do something. If you ignore the headline noise and stay the course, you may wind up better for it - which I will discuss in the following paragraph.

What If You Miss Some Of The Best Days?

A key risk to trading at any time is that you may miss out on strong days that leave you underperforming. Can you miss a pullback or a string of down days? Absolutely - and it sure feels great when you do! But the contrast is also a possibility. If you sell just because the markets hit a high and then they keep going up, you miss out on further gains. And that could have big consequences for your portfolio if you can't time the right moment to get back in.

Let's take the last thirty years as a prime example. If one simply holds through the entire stretch, returns are phenomenal. But look at how dismal they can get if one misses a large number of "best" days in the market:

Of course, staying the course means enduring all the volatility that comes with it. There can be a mental toll to that for sure! But it also means enjoying all the best days, along with the worst. While avoiding the worst days is certainly something that would boost returns, missing the best hurts returns in an opposite fashion. The challenge is that even if you miss some negative days, how likely are you to time the market and get back in to ensure you are holding through the positive days? That's a tough strategy to execute.

The conclusion I draw here is that missing even a handful of strong days can really impact returns significantly. This is why even when the market hits "highs," I am reluctant to make major moves. We simply don't know what is coming next, and just being at a high doesn't necessarily mean drawdowns are on the horizon. If the market continues rallying - and history shows it can - than investors may be doing themselves a big disservice.

Returns Can Be Positive For Multi-Year Stretches

Expanding on the prior point, I want to show that bull runs can often have legs, and that is what makes timing the end of them so difficult. Even during times when we see bear markets (defined as 20% drops or larger), the calendar year returns can still be positive - and often are. In fact, bear markets are typically shorter than bull markets, and that is why staying invested works out in the long run. Just look at the past three years. We have had our share of ups and downs along the way, but the net result is double-digit returns on a multi-year basis, especially for large-cap US stocks (which make up the indices hitting new all-time highs right now):

Now I am not suggesting investors should just blindly accept strong returns every year and never make adjustments. My followers should know me better than that by now! But this is simply a helpful reminder of why timing the market is so difficult. If we use strength (whether fresh highs or just generally strong returns) as reasons to sell, that has a good chance of not being the right move. As the last two and a half years have illustrated, gains can compound on top of gains.

This isn't meant to be a cheerleading article. If investors have gotten too overweight anything - whether it is US stocks or any other sector - than by all means use this opportunity to make some adjustments. Selling into strength is certainly better than selling during weakness. But if one is fine with their allocations, has a generally optimistic view of US markets, and is sitting on gains that would be taxable anyway, then I don't see using this arbitrary "high" in the market as a reason to do anything drastic. If one had done that in 2023, they would have missed out on big gains going forward, as the above graphic shows us.

You Don't Have To Sell To Reduce Volatility

I would reiterate I am not blind to the risks in the market right now. There are always risks or headwinds to be monitored or concerned about, and this market is no different. I would say this whether the US indices had hit highs or not. The market does appear frothy, and many metrics I monitor, including the VIX index, P/E ratios, and investor sentiment, are all signaling it may be time to exercise some caution.

But being cautious doesn't necessarily mean "sell." For example, there are ways to stay in the market or rotate within the market and protect yourself from an uptick in volatility. One strategy that I - as a "Dividend Seeker" - hold near and dear is investing in dividend-paying companies. While receiving the dividends is a nice perk, what really helps to protect one's portfolio is the fact that dividend growers and new payers tend to see lower volatility than the broader market over time:

What this means is steadier returns and a less volatile path along the way. While that may not be the best strategy for everyone, I find it particularly relevant when I'm concerned about short-term moves in the market. With the volatility index at very low levels, it stands to reason we will see an uptick in volatility soon. Holding on to a dividend-paying strategy might help me sleep better at night when this attribute does move higher:

It seems pretty clear to me that sending the volatility index even lower is going to be more challenging than sending it higher. To prepare for this, one doesn't need to sell, but they could build on positions that act as volatility hedges. These include gold, bonds, and - as I stated in this paragraph—dividend-paying companies.

Not To Say There Aren't Risks

Keeping in line with the balancing act of this review, I would remind readers that I view tariffs as a major market headwind that continues to be discounted by the market. We did see a major sell-off when they first came to pass in March and April, but since then the market has shrugged off the implications of current tariffs - and even new ones. This has been rewarding, but it is worth considering that the US tariff rate is elevated compared to history, and that should be having a bigger impact on stock prices in my view:

This is going to cut into corporate profits - the only question is by how much. So far the impact has been minimal, but that may not be the case in the months ahead. Given where markets stand today, it won't take much to rattle them. So I am keeping the ongoing tariff discussions and implementations at the top of my radar, and I suggest to my followers they do the same.

Inflation Doesn't Support Aggressive Rate Cutting

My final point concerns inflation. This has been a topic of discussion since I began investing in a serious manner almost twenty years ago, and I don't see it changing in the years ahead either. Simply put, inflation has subsided in a big way and even leveled off. That is the good news. The shakier news is that it has flatlined at a percentage that is elevated compared to the past decade. While much lower than a few years ago (YOY), it remains well above pre-2021 levels:

The relevance here for me is how this relates to interest rate cuts. The market appears to finally be getting what it has wanted for a long time with the expectation the Fed will begin cutting at its next meeting. This has helped fuel the stock market rally, and investors seem optimistic about the future because of it.

But my concern is investors are getting ahead of themselves. In the same way they predicted rate cuts initiating much sooner than they have, I believe the market is overestimating how many cuts will actually occur. My belief is that inflation is still above the Fed's previously preferred 2% level and that is going to limit just how much action it will take in the short run. Do I see rate cuts ahead? Yes, I do. But I expect the number of them and the pace of them to be more moderate. If I am right, that might challenge the equity market, which has been recently fueled by bets that rate cuts are going to be aggressive.

Bottom Line

The market's bull run continues, and that may have some investors on edge given some of the headline risks facing it. With the indices (in the US) sitting at all-time highs, this may have some wondering if now is the time to sell. While I would not fault anyone for making adjustments to keep their portfolio in line with their own risk tolerance, I would caution against a knee-jerk reaction to sell here.

The market has a tendency to rise over time, and all-time highs in and of themselves do not always signal a top. If one is concerned, there are other strategies to utilize rather than selling, such as building on equity hedges. I am hopeful this review gives readers some comfort as we push into Q4, and I wish everyone well in the weeks and months ahead.

data.title: Vistra Corp.: Visible Earnings Growth With Upside Optionality
data.description:
Investment Action

I give a buy rating for Vistra Corp. (NYSE:VST) as I believe it is uniquely positioned to capture the exponential power demand growth driven by data centers and AI. With a record backlog of opportunities, a visible pipeline of premium contracts, favorable market fundamentals in PJM, and disciplined capital allocation, VST offers investors a mix of near-term catalysts and long-term earnings visibility.

Company Description

VST is a US independent power producer [IPP] and retail electricity provider based in Texas. It operates a large and diversified power generation portfolio of ~41 GW across 20 states, with assets including natural gas, coal, nuclear, solar, and energy storage facilities. VST serves ~5 million retail customers and sells power directly to consumers as well as into wholesale markets. The majority of its generation capacity is located in the ERCOT and PJM markets.

Solid Earnings Growth Outlook

My opinion is that VST is increasingly becoming one of the clearest beneficiaries of the shift in power demand, one that is driven by the exponential growth of data centers and AI. The crux of the bull case is the Comanche Peak nuclear plant in Texas, where management has expressed its strongest conviction yet in securing a landmark contract with a hyperscale customer. This is a massive deal, one that I see as a material growth catalyst. If VST manages to sign a long-term, high-priced contract, it would significantly de-risk one of VST's most important assets and give it a highly visible earnings stream that is insulated from commodity volatility (the cost of uranium is extremely low as a percentage of operating a nuclear plant, and it is mostly long-term contracted). Here is the most important part. Management was explicit that this is not an isolated opportunity, and there is a pipeline of similar discussions, with additional contracts across both nuclear and gas assets potentially being finalized before year-end. Any such value-unlocking agreements would greatly improve the earnings profile and investment case.

The bottleneck to VST growth would be capacity, and in this aspect, VST has worked hard to address it. For example, it has acquired Energy Harbor’s 4 GW nuclear fleet, which gives it clean baseload generation that hyperscale customers demand. It has also acquired Lotus Infrastructure’s gas portfolio, which gives it more scale in the Northeast.

On top of these catalysts, the fundamentals in VST’s core markets are extremely favorable. The PJM capacity market is structurally tight, as we can see from the 2026/27 auction clearing at the cap of ~$329/MWday. This provides an immediate and material uplift to earnings, with a clear line of sight to further upside should the underlying power demand momentum grow. We know this is likely to happen since for the upcoming 2027/28 auction, the system reliability requirement has increased by 6.3 GW, suggesting demand is still very strong and that capacity prices will remain elevated, basically allowing VST to lock in a high-margin revenue stream for the 10.3 GW it has already cleared in PJM.

Put together, VST is in an extremely solid position to capitalize on the surge in power demand, and this makes VST’s growth story compelling.

Capital Allocation

For investors that have looked into the utility sector, capital allocation is always a key part of the investment return equation. And for VST, there isn’t much to worry about. The step up in earnings is translating into free cash flow [FCF], and the conversion rate is improving. VST recently raised its long-term guidance for EBITDA-to-FCF conversion to >60%, up from the previous 55–60% range. Note that it's more than 60%, so it could go a lot higher. The favorable US tax policy changes play a part here (expected to add ~$200 million of incremental FCF annually). Anyway, more FCF means more financial flexibility, which de-risks the balance sheet, gives FCF more funds to invest in growth, and also allows it to pursue an aggressive capital return policy.

On the balance sheet, with the step-up in FCF, VST should be on good track to achieve investment-grade credit status, which is a significant milestone as it would not only lower the cost of capital but also broaden VST's investor base. Combined with EBITDA growth, VST should easily reduce its net debt-to-EBITDA ratio to the 2.2x–2.5x range by 2026. As debt gets paid down and FCF grows, VST would have more FCF left over to buy back shares or issue dividends, which so far has been working well for shareholders (now has $1.4 billion remaining in the current buyback authorization).

Valuation

Using management's FY25 FCF guidance and assuming that FCF grows at half the pace of revenue growth over the past 8 years (i.e., I am not taking into account any new big deals or M&A), at a 20x NTM P/FCF multiple (this is the current multiple), I think VST shares are worth $231. Add on that DPS over the next few years, and we can bridge to low-teens total returns. This is not the best upside profile, but I would think there is a good chance for more big deals and M&As; as such, returns should be more than 13% when they come through. It’s hard to model the timing of this happening, but I think this still deserves a buy rating because of the optionality it brings.

Risk

A key downside risk is the execution and timing of VST data center contracts. I believe the stock's significant run-up has priced in high expectations for a large, near-term deal at its Comanche Peak nuclear facility. Any delays, whether from prolonged negotiations or regulatory hurdles, could disappoint investors, and that may cause near-term share price volatility. Furthermore, if the final terms regarding deal size or pricing fall short of the market's high expectations, it could trigger a sharp sell-off.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for VST. The stock’s upside may not look explosive on base-case assumptions, but optionality from data center contracts and M&A could unlock significant additional value. In my view, the favorable demand backdrop, balance sheet de-risking, and robust free cash flow profile make the equity story very attractive for investors.

data.title: I Couldn't Be More Bullish If I Tried - 3 Energy Stocks To Buy Now
data.description:
Introduction

Guess what?

It’s time to talk about energy stocks again. My most recent in-depth article on this subject wasn’t that long ago, when I wrote "I’m Convinced Energy Is The Most Misunderstood Investment Opportunity Of The Decade" on September 13.

It was one of my favorite articles I have written this month, as I highlighted important things like the fact that energy is one of the cheapest sectors of the S&P 500 on a 10-year basis, even based on current earnings (oil prices). Imagine what these numbers would look like if oil were trading at $75 or higher.

I also brought up how energy stocks are, historically speaking, the best assets to own in an environment of rising inflation. As the chart below shows, energy stocks have the highest historical level of outperformance versus inflation and the highest probability of beating inflation.

However, the biggest focus of the article was on how oil prices enjoy increasingly favorable fundamentals. This discussion is more important than anything else, as the market simply won’t care about attractive energy valuations unless we can expect commodity price tailwinds.

Although cyclical economic growth is still a mess (for lack of a better description), we have been seeing positive developments in the supply/demand balance, which leads me to believe the risk/reward for oil-related (and natural gas) equities is truly fantastic here.

With that said, I am writing this article for two reasons:

  1. We just got new data that confirms that both supply and demand dynamics are much more favorable than one might have expected.
  2. I promised readers I would provide more actionable ideas.

So, let’s get to it!

"I Told You So" - There’s No Way Around Oil

The "I told you so" isn’t coming from me. That’s OPEC (more or less).

Earlier this week, the world’s largest oil cartel published a reaction to the call of the IEA to invest more in oil and gas production. You can access the original IEA report here.

Although I think it’s fair to say IEA has a good reputation overall, it has been one of the leaders in the push for renewable energy and the narrative that oil and gas are becoming a thing of the past. For example, I used the chart below in my latest in-depth energy article. It shows long-term oil demand expectations. All of the IEA’s scenarios see lower oil demand by the year 2050, with Net Zero and pledged scenarios (that’s NZE and APS) seeing a decline in demand of more than 50%.

Now, the EIA has figured out that in order to support even low future demand expectations, the world needs more oil and gas. Here’s an important part from its management summary:

“If current levels of production are to be maintained, over 45 mb/d of oil and around 2,000 bcm of natural gas would be needed in 2050 from new conventional fields.

[...] Filling the remaining supply gap to maintain today’s production through to 2050 would require annual discoveries of 10 billion barrels of oil and around 1,000 bcm of natural gas. These amounts are just above what has been discovered annually in recent years. Developing these resources would add around 18 mb/d and 650 bcm of new oil and gas production by 2050.”

This comes with problems. For example, as I have often brought up in articles, the American Shale Revolution was the main output driver between the Great Financial Crisis and 2021. Now, all major basins except for the Permian have run out of valuable Tier 1 reserves. Meanwhile, the Permian is getting increasingly "gassy," which means a bigger share of output is gas and natural gas liquids instead of crude oil.

Generally speaking, as we can see above, the world will lose half of its oil output without new investments that go beyond sustaining current oil operations. With that said, the only reason I do not expect supply to actually fall is that pricing will do its job. The tighter the supply situation becomes, the higher prices will rise. This stimulates new investments.

In this case, the good news continues, as recent OPEC supply hike decisions have exhausted its excess supply. In my energy article earlier his week, I showed that only Saudi Arabia has excess capacity. I used the quote below, which is key:

If ratified, the group’s decision to start unwinding its next layer of cuts also reflects a tension that has dominated oil markets for months: forecasters are issuing mounting warnings about a looming supply surplus, and yet markets have remained relatively tight over the northern hemisphere summer.

For global oil markets in the longer term, the OPEC+ move serves to erode a longstanding safety net of idle production that could be brought back to cushion unforeseen supply shocks. - Bloomberg

While I’m at it, let me give you another quote from one of my favorite energy analysts on X (formerly known as Twitter), who wrote the following (and posted the chart below):

“In a world where US shale production is in plateau / decline, OPEC spare capacity has now normalized and set to be ~ exhausted, and demand set to grow for DECADES to come, the world is now largely reliant on long-cycle, offshore production. Perhaps a bit scarily:”

Meanwhile, current oil prices are a major headwind for CapEx in the industry, as I already brought up. Irina Slav from Oilprice.com hit the nail on the head when she wrote the following, which shows a pandemic-like scenario for supply investments, something that is very bullish down the road:

Wood Mackenzie has predicted that global capital expenditure on oil and gas exploration is set for a decline of 4.3% this year, for a total of $341.9 billion. The FT cited the research firm as saying this would be the first reduction in global oil and gas exploration spending since 2020, which highlights how serious the situation potentially is in the industry. Not only this, but Big Oil majors would be unable to stick to their current capital spending programs if Brent falls below $60, the FT wrote this week. More importantly for shareholders, they would not be able to stick to their dividend programs. - Oilprice.com

In other words, after a few very painful years for energy executives, investors, and employees in the sector, I believe oil is bottoming, as cyclical demand is likely improving with rate cuts and deregulation, secular growth from massive data center demand is requiring every energy molecule, and increasing supply tightness adds another bullish layer.

Buying Capital Gains Potential And High-Quality Income Through 3 Of My Favorite Stocks

Now comes my favorite part. As much as I enjoyed writing the first part of this article, discussing actionable ideas is always more fun, as it allows us to actually profit from our theses. Assuming they come to fruition, of course.

The first stock I want to present is Viper Energy (VNOM). This company is a spin-off that is still managed by its partner, Diamondback Energy (FANG). Viper Energy owns more than 61 thousand net royalty acres in the Permian Basin, where it allows companies like FANG, which accounts for 60% of its operations, to produce oil and gas in exchange for royalties.

Its business model is super straightforward. Instead of producing oil and gas itself, it simply lets others do it. Because it receives royalties, it does not have to spend capital on production, which gives it an ultra-high margin profile.

For example, its base dividend, which currently yields 3.4%, is breakeven below $30 WTI, a number that not a single oil producer on my radar can match. They don’t even come close.

Moreover, the company, which maintains a 75% payout ratio through quarterly special dividends and buybacks, has a payout ratio of a mere 25% at $80 WTI. As a result, even at $75 WTI, investors who buy at current prices can achieve a yield of more than 8.0%.

Moreover, because the company has no CapEx requirements (it doesn’t even have employees), it comes with the benefit of additional inflation protection.

After all, rising costs won’t hurt its expenses as much as the expenses of oil and gas drillers, which gives it another edge.

If I had a bigger focus on income, I would own VNOM instead of having a 25% position in LandBridge (LB) and Texas Pacific Land (TPL), which are not part of this article because they both yield less than 1.0%.

This brings me to Tourmaline Oil (TOU:CA), another perfect mix of income and capital gains potential. In this case, we’re dealing with a Canadian company. Although the company has "Oil" in its name, it’s Canada’s largest natural gas producer. It is also the fourth-largest gas processing midstream operator, as it has a lot of midstream operations "in-house."

On top of that, it has a low-cost drilling inventory of 75 years. This excludes any new future discoveries in the future and is one of the best reserve profiles among all energy companies I have ever covered (I have covered many!).

Because of its strong midstream network and deep reserves, the company is in a great spot to boost its output, especially when it comes to higher-margin liquids. As we can see below, the company is Canada’s 4th-largest producer of conventional liquids. By 2031, it aims to produce 200 thousand barrels of natural gas liquids and other liquids per day. That would be nearly 100% more than it produced in 2022.

As we can see below, this year’s total production is expected to be 640,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day. By 2031, that number is expected to be 850,000. My Casio calculator tells me that’s 33% growth. By then, it also expects to generate C$2.9 billion in free cash flow, 12% of its market cap. Needless to say, pricing tailwinds could substantially improve that number.

This is great news for its dividend.

As we can see below, Tourmaline pays eight dividends per year. This includes four regular quarterly dividends and four special quarterly dividends. The current total payout yield is 5.5%, which is a terrific number in an environment of oil in the low-$60 range and American natural gas prices close to $3.00 Henry Hub. In the future, the company expects to keep returning the majority of its free cash flow to shareholders.

Given my view on oil and gas, I think Tourmaline is in a prime spot to generate tremendous shareholder value for many years to come, which is why I see it as a Strong Buy.

The same goes for the next stock, which is a lot more speculative than the first two picks.

Permian Resources (PR) is a company I started covering last year. It’s a relatively new company, as it’s the result of a 2022 merger between Centennial Resource Development and Colgate Energy. Since then, it has made more deals, including the 2023 takeover of Earthstone Energy for $4.5 billion.

Now, it’s a pure-play producer in the Delaware Basin, one of the two sub-basins of the Permian Basin. Here, it operates on nearly 500,000 net acres, where it produces 385 thousand barrels of oil equivalent per day with a high-quality inventory life of more than 15 years. These stats alone lead me to believe that PR is a prime takeover target.

Even better, more than 70% of its production mix consists of oil and high-margin liquids, which is a huge benefit in the increasingly gassy Permian, as I already mentioned.

On top of that, it has a stellar balance sheet, which is part of its Downturn Playbook. Currently, it has a net leverage ratio of a mere 1.0x, which it can get down to 0.8x at $60 WTI, a number that is truly impressive. Because of this balance sheet, it is buying back stock while its equity is cheap, something that will help it boost shareholder value during upswings.

Moreover, on top of being upgraded to BBB- by Fitch (it’s now investment grade), the company’s dividend is breakeven at roughly $40 WTI. That’s impressive, as its base dividend yields 4.5%.

On top of that, the company is increasingly improving its pricing power by avoiding the Permian’s Waha Hub, which has the worst natural gas prices in all of the U.S. because of the enormous growth rates of associated natural gas in the area.

Permian Resources is shifting more volumes to the Gulf Coast and Central/East Texas. This improves pricing and allows it to become a bigger player as a source for LNG feedstock and data center power, something I expect to become a huge business in the Permian Basin.

Moreover, the company trades way below its bigger peers. At roughly 4.0x EBITDAX, I believe it trades at least 25% below the 5.0x multiple that’s warranted for its portfolio, regardless of a potential "size discount."

In general, PR is super cheap, as it trades at just 2.7x operating cash flow (FactSet data). That’s below its own post-merger average of 5.2x, and a mile below the 8-10x multiple I usually apply to high-quality oil stocks.

It's also why I expect the next oil upswing to take PR way beyond its prior all-time high.

The only reason I do not own any of these stocks is that I have 25% exposure to LandBridge and Texas Pacific Land. I also own Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ). If I did not run a concentrated 16-stock portfolio, I would own all the stocks mentioned in this article.

Takeaway
